stem: A factory receives parts from two suppliers. Supplier A provides \( \frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]} \) of all parts, and Supplier B provides the rest. Historically, \( \frac{[[dAn]]}{[[dAd]]} \) of Supplier A's parts are defective, while \( \frac{[[dBn]]}{[[dBd]]} \) of Supplier B's parts are defective. A part is selected at random from the combined stock and found to be defective. What is the probability that it came from Supplier A?
solution: By Bayes' theorem, \( P(A\mid \text{def}) = \dfrac{P(A)P(\text{def}\mid A)}{P(A)P(\text{def}\mid A) + P(B)P(\text{def}\mid B)} = \dfrac{\frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]}\cdot\frac{[[dAn]]}{[[dAd]]}}{\frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]}\cdot\frac{[[dAn]]}{[[dAd]]} + \left(1-\frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]}\right)\cdot\frac{[[dBn]]}{[[dBd]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just Supplier A's defect rate, ignoring the prior probabilities entirely.
This is just the prior probability that a part came from A, ignoring the defect rates.
This is only the numerator of Bayes' theorem; it forgets to divide by the total probability of a defect.
This computes \( P(B\mid\text{def}) \), the complementary posterior, instead of \( P(A\mid\text{def}) \).
This weights both suppliers' defect rates equally instead of by their production shares.
This forgets to weight Supplier B's defect rate by its own prior probability in the denominator.

stem: In a population, the prevalence of a disease is \( \frac{[[preN]]}{[[preD]]} \). A diagnostic test has sensitivity \( \frac{[[sensN]]}{[[sensD]]} \) (the probability of a positive result given the disease is present) and specificity \( \frac{[[specN]]}{[[specD]]} \) (the probability of a negative result given the disease is absent). A randomly selected person from this population tests positive. What is the probability that this person actually has the disease?
solution: By Bayes' theorem, \( P(\text{disease}\mid +) = \dfrac{P(\text{disease})\,P(+\mid\text{disease})}{P(\text{disease})P(+\mid\text{disease}) + P(\text{no disease})P(+\mid\text{no disease})} = \dfrac{\frac{[[preN]]}{[[preD]]}\cdot\frac{[[sensN]]}{[[sensD]]}}{\frac{[[preN]]}{[[preD]]}\cdot\frac{[[sensN]]}{[[sensD]]} + \left(1-\frac{[[preN]]}{[[preD]]}\right)\left(1-\frac{[[specN]]}{[[specD]]}\right)} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the test's sensitivity, ignoring how rare the disease is.
This is just the disease's prevalence, ignoring the test result entirely.
This is only the numerator of Bayes' theorem, forgetting to divide by the total probability of testing positive.
This confuses specificity with the false-positive rate, using \( \text{specificity} \) instead of \( 1-\text{specificity} \) for the false-positive probability.
This computes \( P(\text{no disease}\mid +) \), the complementary posterior, instead of \( P(\text{disease}\mid +) \).
This multiplies sensitivity and specificity together, an unrelated quantity.

stem: Three machines A, B, and C produce items in the ratio \( [[n1]]:[[n2]]:[[n3]] \). Machine A's defect rate is \( \frac{[[d1n]]}{[[dd]]} \), Machine B's is \( \frac{[[d2n]]}{[[dd]]} \), and Machine C's is \( \frac{[[d3n]]}{[[dd]]} \). An item is chosen at random from the combined output and found to be defective. What is the probability that it came from Machine B?
solution: By Bayes' theorem with three sources, \( P(B\mid\text{def}) = \dfrac{n_B\,p_B}{n_A p_A + n_B p_B + n_C p_C} = \dfrac{[[n2]]\cdot\frac{[[d2n]]}{[[dd]]}}{[[n1]]\cdot\frac{[[d1n]]}{[[dd]]} + [[n2]]\cdot\frac{[[d2n]]}{[[dd]]} + [[n3]]\cdot\frac{[[d3n]]}{[[dd]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just Machine B's defect rate, ignoring the production ratio entirely.
This is just Machine B's share of production, ignoring the defect rates.
This computes the posterior probability for Machine A instead of Machine B.
This computes the posterior probability for Machine C instead of Machine B.
This is only the numerator, forgetting to divide by the total probability of a defect across all three machines.
This weights each machine's defect rate equally instead of by its production share.

stem: Among a group of people, [[n11]] have a certain condition and test positive for it, [[n12]] have the condition and test negative, [[n21]] do not have the condition but test positive, and [[n22]] do not have the condition and test negative. A person from this group is selected at random and is found to have tested positive. What is the probability that this person actually has the condition?
solution: Restrict attention to the positive-testing subgroup: \( P(\text{condition}\mid +) = \dfrac{n_{11}}{n_{11}+n_{21}} = \dfrac{[[n11]]}{[[n11]]+[[n21]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by the condition-present total instead of the positive-test total; that computes sensitivity, not the requested posterior.
This divides by the grand total of everyone, giving a joint probability rather than a conditional one.
This is the complementary posterior \( P(\text{no condition}\mid +) \).
This computes an unrelated ratio among the negative-testing subgroup.
This divides by the grand total instead of just the positive-testing subgroup, computing the marginal probability of testing positive.
This divides by the wrong subgroup total (condition-present) using the negative-test count.

stem: Two competing hypotheses \( H_1 \) and \( H_2 \) have prior probabilities in the ratio \( P(H_1):P(H_2) = [[priorA]]:[[priorB]] \). A new piece of evidence \( E \) is observed, with likelihood ratio \( P(E\mid H_1):P(E\mid H_2) = [[likeA]]:[[likeB]] \). Using Bayes' theorem in odds form, what is the posterior probability \( P(H_1\mid E) \)?
solution: The posterior odds are the prior odds times the likelihood ratio: \( \dfrac{P(H_1\mid E)}{P(H_2\mid E)} = \dfrac{[[priorA]]}{[[priorB]]}\cdot\dfrac{[[likeA]]}{[[likeB]]} \). Converting odds \( [[priorA]]\cdot[[likeA]]:[[priorB]]\cdot[[likeB]] \) to a probability gives \( P(H_1\mid E) = \dfrac{[[priorA]]\cdot[[likeA]]}{[[priorA]]\cdot[[likeA]]+[[priorB]]\cdot[[likeB]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the prior odds, ignoring the new evidence's likelihood ratio.
This uses only the likelihood ratio, ignoring the prior probabilities.
This computes the complementary posterior \( P(H_2\mid E) \) instead.
This swaps which likelihood term multiplies which prior term.
This adds the odds components instead of multiplying prior and likelihood terms.
This reports the posterior ODDS ratio itself rather than converting it to a probability.

stem: A factory receives parts from two suppliers. Supplier A provides \( \frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]} \) of all parts, and Supplier B provides the rest. Historically, \( \frac{[[dAn]]}{[[dAd]]} \) of Supplier A's parts are defective, while \( \frac{[[dBn]]}{[[dBd]]} \) of Supplier B's parts are defective. What is the overall probability that a randomly selected part from the combined stock is defective, regardless of supplier?
solution: By the law of total probability, \( P(\text{def}) = P(A)P(\text{def}\mid A) + P(B)P(\text{def}\mid B) = \frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]}\cdot\frac{[[dAn]]}{[[dAd]]} + \left(1-\frac{[[pAn]]}{[[pAd]]}\right)\cdot\frac{[[dBn]]}{[[dBd]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two defect rates without weighting them by each supplier's production share.
This is just Supplier A's defect rate, ignoring Supplier B entirely.
This averages the two defect rates unweighted, ignoring the actual production split.
This computes the Bayes POSTERIOR \( P(A\mid\text{def}) \) instead of the marginal probability of a defect.
This swaps which defect rate is paired with which supplier's share.
This adds the production share and both defect rates together, which mixes incompatible quantities.
